data.title: Linde outlines $16.30–$16.50 full-year EPS target as project backlog and cash flow hit record levels
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Linde plc (LIN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sanjiv Lamba highlighted that "EPS of $4.09 and operating margin of 30.1%, both represent all-time quarterly highs, against the backdrop of a challenging macro environment." He noted operating cash flows grew 15% and ROC reached 25.1%. Lamba focused on the project backlog, stating, "In a little over 4 years, the sale of gas backlog has approximately doubled, from $3.6 billion to $7.1 billion," emphasizing that almost three-quarters of this backlog is in the Americas, mainly for electronics and clean energy projects.
  • Lamba announced the addition of the Blue Point project, a joint venture in Louisiana, as a significant clean energy contract, bringing the total to approximately $5 billion in large clean energy wins. He also discussed a new merchant investment in the Southeastern U.S. supporting space launches and mentioned that tuck-in acquisitions contributed a 1% top line increase in the quarter.
  • CFO Matthew J. White stated, "Sales of $8.5 billion increased 3% over prior year and 5% sequentially. Year-over-year FX headwinds abated as we saw a 3% sequential improvement from broad-based weakening of the U.S. dollar." White also noted, "Operating profit of $2.6 billion increased 6% over prior year. The operating margin of 30.1% increased 80 basis points or 100 basis points when excluding the effect of cost pass-through. EPS of $4.09 also increased 6% from prior year as a lower share count was mostly offset by a higher effective tax rate."

Outlook

  • White provided Q3 guidance: "For the third quarter, we're providing a guidance range of $4.10 to $4.20, or 4% to 7% above last year. This includes an assumed 1% currency tailwind, which would be the first quarterly FX benefit since late 2023."
  • For the full year, White said, "This resulted in a new range of $16.30 to $16.50, or 5% to 6% growth, including a 1% currency tailwind."
  • White explained the guidance approach as updating improved FX but offsetting with an assumption of economic contraction at the top end.

Financial Results

  • Linde reported sales of $8.5 billion, operating profit of $2.6 billion, and operating cash flow growth of 15% for the quarter. Operating margin reached 30.1%, and EPS was $4.09.
  • Acquisitions lifted sales 1% over prior year, and underlying sales grew 1% over prior year and 3% sequentially. Volumes were down 1% from last year, mainly due to weaker base volumes in EMEA.
  • White stated, "The pie chart represents a steady and disciplined capital allocation policy, which deployed almost $6.5 billion year-to-date. Of this, $2.8 billion comprises investments that met our risk-reward criteria, an increase of 20% over last year."
  • Linde issued CHF 0.5 billion in bonds with an average yield less than 1% during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Patrick Duffy Fischer, Goldman Sachs, asked about geographic and end market outlook. Lamba responded that in the Americas, "growth in the resilient end market" offsets a softer industrial sector and expressed confidence in the U.S. market, whereas Europe is expected to see continued softening with "no catalyst for economic improvement this year." Asia remains mixed, with India as a bright spot.
  • David L. Begleiter, Deutsche Bank, inquired about pricing risks. Lamba stated, "over the last 25 years, Praxair-Linde has always achieved positive pricing... and that remains the expectation going forward as well."
  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley, questioned margin dynamics. White noted, "almost 32% margins we're tracking. We clearly see more room to improve."
  • Laurent Favre, BNP Paribas, asked about project backlog risks. Lamba reiterated, "my expectation remains that we will end the year with a backlog with a 7 handle on it..."
  • Jeffrey John Zekauskas, JPMorgan, questioned EBIT growth and helium impact. White attributed EMEA EBIT growth to FX, pricing, and productivity, and Lamba noted, "our exposure on helium is very different and much smaller... year-to-date, our helium volumes are flat."
  • Other questions addressed European volume risks, growth in the commercial space sector, return profiles of backlog projects, electronics, energy transition, base volume guidance, and the impact of U.S. tax policy changes.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a cautious but constructive tone, frequently asking about geographic volume trends, pricing sustainability, and backlog risks. They pressed for specifics on European and Chinese outlooks, margin sustainability, and project execution amid macro uncertainty.
  • Management displayed confidence in their pricing power, backlog turnover, and capital allocation. Phrases like "I'm highly confident" and "I'm certain Linde will remain the undisputed leader" signaled optimism. However, White tempered expectations, saying, "currency volatility and general economic uncertainty don't give us enough confidence to raise the outlook at this time."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a similarly guarded but proactive tone, while analysts continued to probe for downside risks and execution clarity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 and the full year shifted to include a currency tailwind and an explicit economic contraction assumption at the top end, compared to last quarter's offsetting of improved FX with negative volume assumptions.
  • Project backlog remains robust, while management highlighted increased project turnover and execution. Analysts maintained focus on geographic weakness, especially Europe, and the sustainability of margin and pricing gains.
  • Management's confidence in U.S. market growth and space sector opportunities increased, with more discussion on base CapEx and tuck-in acquisitions. Analysts' skepticism about Europe and project timing persisted.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Lamba described Europe as facing "several challenges to get their economy back on their feet" and noted no current catalyst for improvement. He acknowledged the risk of further volume declines in the region.
  • Management cited economic uncertainty, currency volatility, and weak industrial demand as ongoing headwinds. Lamba highlighted that the negative volume headwinds are "a function of contractual customers taking less gas due to the economic uncertainty."
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned potential delays or slippage in project backlog and the risk to future growth in Europe and China.

Final Takeaway

Linde's Q2 2025 call emphasized record project backlog, strong cash flow, and robust EPS and margin performance despite a challenging global environment. Management reaffirmed its disciplined approach to project investment, pricing, and capital allocation, signaling confidence in capturing growth from resilient end markets, U.S. and APAC expansion, and emerging sectors such as commercial space. While full-year EPS guidance was raised to $16.30–$16.50, the company remains cautious about macroeconomic conditions, particularly in Europe, but continues to expect its operational resilience and project execution to drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cinemark targets $225M in capex for 2025 as box office momentum fuels record revenue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cinemark Holdings, Inc. (CNK) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sean Gamble reported "the second quarter launched out of the gates with the record-breaking results of a Minecraft movie, which has now generated over $950 million in global box office proceeds." He described a surge in North American industry box office to $2.7 billion, up more than 35% year-over-year, flipping year-to-date tracking from a 12% deficit to a 14% gain by the end of June. Gamble highlighted a "steady stream of highly compelling new releases" and cited consumer enthusiasm with "audiences turned up in mass for films like the genre defying breakout hit centers, the high-flying live-action remake of How to Train Your Dragon and Marvel's most recent Avenger Adventure, Thunderbolts." He emphasized the company’s "highest quarterly domestic admissions revenues since the pandemic and our third highest quarterly result of all time."
  • Gamble pointed to the success of strategic initiatives, stating, "we continue to realize upside from our efforts to take full advantage of nontraditional programming opportunities which drove more than 10% of our admissions revenues for the fourth straight quarter in a row." The CEO also noted market share gains, the impact of family titles, and loyalty program growth, with Movie Club reaching 1.45 million members, up 12% year-over-year.
  • CFO Melissa Thomas stated, "Our robust second quarter results reflect a compelling film slate that clearly resonated with audiences as well as the successful execution of our strategic initiatives to drive revenue and efficiently scale our operations." She noted, "In the second quarter, global attendance grew 16% year-over-year to 57.9 million patrons, and worldwide revenue increased 28% to $940.5 million. We delivered $232.2 million of adjusted EBITDA and expanded our adjusted EBITDA margin by 530 basis points to 24.7%, reflecting increased operating leverage driven by the attendance growth in the quarter as well as improved monetization and productivity advancements."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated expectations for "capital expenditures of approximately $225 million" for the full year. Thomas explained that capex remains "back-half weighted" and is focused on "maintain[ing] and enhance[ing] our high-quality circuit."
  • Thomas confirmed expectations for "modest growth year-over-year for full year 2025" in average ticket prices and "moderate year-over-year growth in our domestic per cap for full year" driven by ongoing initiatives and strategic pricing.
  • On cash taxes, Thomas highlighted the expected positive impact from new tax legislation but said, "it's still premature to provide quantification there. We do expect our cash taxes to meaningfully benefit from the new legislation, particularly as it relates to the 100% bonus depreciation and the loosening of the interest expense limitation."

Financial Results

  • Cinemark reported $940.5 million in worldwide revenue for the second quarter. Adjusted EBITDA was $232.2 million, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 24.7%.
  • Domestic revenue reached $759.3 million, with $383.4 million in admissions revenue and $307.6 million in concession revenue. Concession per cap hit a record $8.34.
  • International revenue was $181.2 million. Global net income attributable to Cinemark Holdings was $93.5 million, resulting in diluted earnings per share of $0.63. Free cash flow for the quarter was $246 million. Thomas detailed, "We ended the quarter with $932 million of cash as we prepare to address our convertible notes later this month."
  • Film rental and advertising expense increased to 58% of admissions revenue, and concession costs as a percentage of concession revenue were 19.4%.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Daniel Swinburne, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the convertible note settlement and capital allocation. Thomas responded, "our current intent is to settle the warrants and shares... Ultimately, our decision will be contingent upon the extent to which the stock price exceeds $22, our cash and liquidity and potential dilution considerations, among other factors."
  • Swinburne also inquired about the cash flow benefits of new tax legislation. Thomas replied that while quantification is premature, "we do expect our cash taxes to meaningfully benefit from the new legislation."
  • David Karnovsky, JPMorgan: Asked about the PLF strategy and co-branding. Gamble explained, "PLF still only account for about 15% of domestic box office... there are numerous fantastic PLF experiences...including IMAX, which is a tremendous experience, clearly."
  • Eric Owen Handler, Roth MKM: Asked about discount Wednesdays and merchandise. Gamble said, "We've had a very successful discount Tuesday program at Cinemark for many years... Overall, our decisions are really based on elasticity of demand and consumer value perception as we approach price." On merchandise, Gamble noted year-over-year merch was up close to 240% but remains a small overall portion of food and beverage.
  • Chad C. Beynon, Macquarie: Inquired about content in the back half of the year and international performance. Gamble confirmed a strong Q4 slate and noted "overall performance, and we are still thrilled with the results of LatAm basically with flat attendance year-over-year margins in excess of 24%."
  • Drew Crum, B. Riley Securities: Asked about average ticket price and per cap guidance. Thomas reiterated expectations for modest growth in both metrics for the full year, with quarter-to-quarter fluctuations depending on film mix.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was largely constructive but focused on specifics around capital allocation, margin sustainability, and growth in new revenue streams.
  • Management’s tone was confident and detailed, frequently highlighting record performances and strong execution. Gamble emphasized, "we remain highly encouraged about the film pipeline ahead, and we believe Cinemark remains exceptionally well positioned."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management were more upbeat, with analysts less focused on risk and more on growth and capital returns.
  • The prepared remarks reflected a high level of confidence, while management remained transparent in responses, particularly around cost and capital allocation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter marks a significant turnaround from Q1, with revenue increasing from $540.7 million to $940.5 million and adjusted EBITDA rising from $36.4 million to $232.2 million.
  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism in Q1 to a more assertive outlook on content momentum and capital spending.
  • Analysts in Q2 centered questions on shareholder returns, capital structure, and operational leverage, whereas Q1 focused more on recovery and lingering strike impacts.
  • Management’s confidence increased, supported by "highest quarterly domestic admissions revenues since the pandemic" and robust market share retention.
  • Strategic priorities now emphasize expanding premium formats, loyalty programs, and operational productivity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Film rental and advertising expenses increased, attributed to "increased concentration of high-grossing films and higher marketing spend."
  • Concession costs remain subject to inflationary pressures and product mix shifts.
  • Management flagged deferred maintenance costs, with about half of the budgeted $8-10 million already incurred.
  • International performance may diverge due to "tougher comp year-over-year" and film slate timing differences.
  • Capital returns are partly contingent on the resolution of the convertible notes and warrants.

Final Takeaway

Cinemark delivered record revenue and operating results in Q2 2025, driven by a robust film slate, strategic pricing, and expansion of premium offerings and loyalty programs. Management remains confident in sustaining market share gains and is committed to disciplined capital allocation, with $225 million in capex planned for the year and a focus on operational productivity, while monitoring inflationary pressures and capital structure events in the months ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Eastman Chemical plunges 19%, S&P 500's biggest loser, on dismal guidance
data.description:
Eastman Chemical (NYSE:EMN) finished Friday's trading -19%, making it the biggest loser on the S&P 500, after missing Q2 adjusted earnings expectations and announcing plans to cut inventory in the face of weak demand and uncertain U.S. trade policy.

Citing an expected decline in volume due to trade dispute impacts and normal seasonality, Eastman (NYSE:EMN) said it plans to increase its focus on generating cash as it reduces inventory by more than $200M below current levels, which is seen knocking $75M-$100M off earnings for the rest of the year, including ~$50M in the current quarter.

Eastman (EMN) also forecast Q2 adjusted earnings of $1.25/share, well below the $1.91/share analyst consensus estimate provided by FactSet, and now sees full-year operating cash flow coming in at ~$1B, compared to previous guidance of $1.2B, after posting operating cash flow of $1.29B in 2024 and $1.37B in 2023.

While the company said it is in "a solid financial position in an industry that is going on four years of significant challenges," it is also "encountering a global macroeconomic environment that remains challenging," CEO Mark Costa said. "Customer caution is intensifying due to a changing tariff landscape and weak underlying demand."

data.title: Kansas City Life Insurance reports Q2 results
data.description: • Kansas City Life Insurance press release (OTC:KCLI): Q2 net loss of $28.1 million or $2.90 per share.

data.title: DIH receives deficiency letter from Nasdaq
data.description:
• DIH (NASDAQ:DHAI) on Friday said it received a Nasdaq notice, stating that the company was not in compliance with Nasdaq Listing Rule as it had not filed its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the period ended March 31, 2025, with the Securities and Exchange Commission.
  • Under Nasdaq rules, the company has 60 calendar days from the date of the notice to file its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the period ended March 31, 2025. If the company is unable to file the Annual Report by that date, the company is permitted to submit a plan of compliance on or prior to that date. 
  • The notice has no immediate impact on the listing or trading of the Company’s securities on Nasdaq.
  • DHAI -19.77% after hours to $0.2.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Sagen MI Canada reports Q2 results

• Andlauer Healthcare press release (OTCPK:ANDHF): Q2 Revenue of C$156.6M beats by C$33.77M.

data.title: Smart Share Global enters into definitive merger agreement for going private transaction
data.description:
• Smart Share Global (NASDAQ:EM) has entered into a definitive agreement and plan of merger with Mobile Charging Group, Mobile Charging Investment and Mobile Charging Merger in a transaction implying an equity value of the company of about $327 million in which the company will be acquired by a consortium of investors.
  • The consortium includes Trustar Mobile Charging; Mars Guangyuan Cai, chairman of the board and CEO of the company;  Peifeng Xu, director and president of the company; Victor Yaoyu Zhang, chief marketing officer and Maria Yi Xin, director and chief financial officer of the company.
  • The merger consideration represents a premium of 74.8% to the closing trading price of the ADSs on January 3, 2025, the last trading day prior to the company’s receipt of the going private proposal.
  • The consortium intends to fund the merger through a combination of cash contributions from certain members to their respective equity commitment letters, proceeds from certain committed term loan facility to be provided by Bank of China, Shanghai Branch, and rollover equity contributions by the Rollover Shareholders.
  • The merger is currently expected to close during the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Gerdau signals post-2026 CapEx reduction and prioritizes North America amid Brazil import challenges
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Gerdau S.A. (GGB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gustavo Werneck Da Cunha highlighted a record low in workplace accident rates and noted, "we ended the second quarter of '25 with another positive milestone in our historical series of workplace accident rates, reinforcing our commitment to the health, well-being and safety of our employees and all stakeholders with whom we interact."
  • Werneck emphasized the "strong recovery of our North American operations, which in the second quarter posted the highest all-time share in our results, representing 61% of consolidated EBITDA."
  • He addressed the ongoing challenges in Brazil, stating, "the domestic market continued to be impacted by excessive imports of steel throughout the second quarter," and announced a strategic shift: "Given this scenario...we decided to reduce our investments in Brazil and the details of which we will announce over the coming months."
  • CFO Rafael Dorneles Japur reported, "Our adjusted EBITDA was BRL 2.6 billion, 6.6% higher than in the first quarter of 2025, with the North America segment's performance standing out...Net income stood at BRL 864 million or BRL 0.43 per share, up 14% compared to the first quarter of '25."
  • Japur also detailed capital structure moves: "During this first quarter, we issued 2 important debt issuance, a USD 650 million bond maturing in 2035 and a 7-year debenture totaling BRL 1.4 billion."
  • CapEx investment was highlighted by Japur: "We invested BRL 1.6 billion in the quarter, where we allocated most of it in our Miguel Burnier sustainable mining project, which has already reached 72% completion."

Outlook

  • Werneck indicated, "for the coming months, we see a resilient civil construction market," but warned of ongoing risks from high import levels in Brazil and closely monitored impacts on the automotive and agricultural sectors.
  • The CEO confirmed a strategic reduction: "a decision has been made to reduce the global CapEx disbursement from 2026 and beyond...after 2026, a decision has been made to reduce the level of disbursements."
  • Japur reiterated that "the BRL 6 billion we announced of disbursement for this year will be fully executed. But after 2026, a decision has been made to reduce the level of disbursements."
  • The company expects "an important part of this BRL 1.1 billion will start being captured along 2026" from the Miguel Burnier project.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBITDA reached BRL 2.6 billion, up 6.6% quarter-over-quarter, with North America contributing 61% of consolidated EBITDA.
  • Net income was BRL 864 million or BRL 0.43 per share, a 14% increase from Q1 2025.
  • CapEx for the quarter totaled BRL 1.6 billion, with the majority allocated to the Miguel Burnier mining project.
  • The company approved a dividend distribution of BRL 0.12 per share, totaling BRL 239 million, and continued its 2025 share buyback program, achieving 68% completion representing 2.2% of outstanding shares.
  • The payout ratio for the quarter was 90%.
  • Leverage ratio ended the period at 0.85x net debt over EBITDA.

Q&A

  • Lucas Laghi, XP: Asked about CapEx expectations and capital allocation amid differing market contexts. Werneck explained the decision to reduce CapEx post-2026, prioritizing North American investments, while Japur provided assumptions for the Miguel Burnier project, highlighting its expected BRL 1.1 billion incremental EBITDA.
  • Leonardo Correa, BTG Pactual: Questioned net debt increase and market strategy for rebar pricing. Japur attributed leverage growth to shareholder returns and ongoing CapEx, while Werneck emphasized maintaining market share and acknowledged challenges from imports, noting, "profitability, margins and prices are below what they should be."
  • Daniel Sasson, Itau BBA: Inquired about gross debt policy and structural measures. Werneck explained cash position nuances due to regional differences and confirmed 1,500 dismissals in 2025, with ongoing reviews of structural responses to Brazil's competitive pressures.
  • Rafael Barcellos, Bradesco BBI: Probed the rebar market and CapEx balance. Japur stated, "it makes no sense for us to give out any market share," and clarified that maintenance CapEx would continue even as growth investments in Brazil are reduced.
  • Rodolfo R. De Angele, JPMorgan: Sought details on cost reduction in Brazil and U.S. strategy. Japur outlined cost dilution opportunities as production normalizes, while Werneck described a strong U.S. demand pipeline in sectors like solar and data centers and noted the strategic postponement of downtime at the Midlothian facility to meet solid demand.
  • Gabriel Barra, Citi: Asked about capital allocation with higher expected cash generation. Werneck prioritized share buybacks, stating, "our #1 priority in the current context would be to have share buybacks," and anticipated significant EBITDA growth from maturing projects.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a critical and probing tone, pressing management on capital allocation, leverage, and market competitiveness, particularly regarding Brazil’s exposure to imports and the rationale for debt and payout levels.
  • Management was confident yet defensive, especially when addressing Brazil’s challenges, using phrases like "we understand that this is something temporary" and "the decision has been made."
  • Compared to last quarter, analyst skepticism on Brazil’s prospects increased, while management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to firmer strategic realignment toward North America.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most significant change was the explicit announcement of post-2026 CapEx reduction, focused on Brazil, compared to prior guidance that only hinted at potential lower future disbursements.
  • North America’s contribution to consolidated EBITDA reached a record 61%, versus last quarter’s noted recovery.
  • Brazil’s import pressures intensified, leading to concrete strategic shifts, whereas previously management awaited governmental action.
  • Analysts shifted focus from general capital allocation to more pointed questions on leverage, cost structure, and the sustainability of dividends and buybacks.
  • Management’s confidence in North America increased, with more detail on backlog strength and sector opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Persistent high steel imports in Brazil continue to erode domestic profitability and threaten future investments.
  • Management is monitoring potential impacts from U.S. tariffs on Brazil and the risk of further import surges.
  • Short-term cost inflation and production disruptions in Brazil, tied to ongoing CapEx projects, were identified but are expected to ease as projects ramp up.
  • Analysts raised concerns related to elevated debt, cash generation, and the sustainability of high payout ratios amid adverse Brazilian market dynamics.

Final Takeaway

Management reinforced that Gerdau’s resilience in Q2 2025 was underpinned by record performance in North America, and the company is executing a decisive shift in capital allocation, reducing future investments in Brazil while maintaining and expanding in the U.S. Key projects in mining and flat steel will drive incremental EBITDA as they ramp up, and capital returns to shareholders remain a priority through buybacks and dividends. Strategic cost management and a strong North American outlook offset ongoing pressures in Brazil, setting a new direction for the company’s growth and investment focus beyond 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
